extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A thermal incubator system comprising an insulated styrofoam container (0.03 m³ volume), a heatbed (214×214×3 mm) for uniform heating, a fan (0.02 m³/s airflow) for air circulation, three DS18S20 temperature sensors (-55°C to +125°C, 0.5°C accuracy), and a Raspberry Pi 4 Model B controller. The system regulates temperature within the insulated container regardless of internal content, with a target operational domain of maintaining stable thermal conditions in a controlled environment. Key characteristics include thermal inertia prediction, energy/airflow requirements estimation, and integration of cyber-physical components.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Monitoring (real-time temperature tracking via sensors), prediction (thermal inertia analysis using 2-parameter and 4-parameter simulation models), control (Raspberry Pi-based temperature regulation with fan/heatbed actuation), and optimization (calibration algorithms for model parameter estimation). The DT enables simulation of physical twin behavior under operational conditions, supports fault detection through data analysis, and facilitates state estimation for system safety verification.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Raspberry Pi 4 Model B (controller with GPIO for sensor/fan/heatbed interface), RabbitMQ message broker (for communication server and controller message passing), DS18S20 1-Wire temperature sensors (64-bit unique IDs), MOSFETs with optocouplers (for electrical isolation), PCB schematic design (fig. 4), low-level drivers (sensor/actuator abstraction), and open-source simulation models (2-parameter and 4-parameter thermal prediction). RabbitMQ enables real-time data synchronization, while the PCB design decouples control signals from high-current loads.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time synchronization between physical and digital components via RabbitMQ message broker, with Kalman filter state estimation and self-adaptation loop (steps 1-8 in section 5.3). Calibration experiments use continuous data collection for model parameter updates. Temporal granularity includes 1-second intervals for temperature sampling (DS18S20 sensor) and 2-second air circulation cycles (fan airflow).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Dual model instances (Model A with 2 parameters vs Model B with 4 parameters) for thermal prediction. Centralized architecture with Raspberry Pi as controller hub, coordinating 3 temperature sensors, 1 fan, and heatbed actuators. Hierarchical structure includes low-level drivers, communication server, and controller layers with distinct responsibilities.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestrated integration of modeling (calibrated thermal parameters), simulation (co-simulations with historical/real-time data), monitoring (anomaly detection via Kalman filter), and optimization (controller parameter tuning). Architecture patterns include message-passing between controller/load-level driver components, with RabbitMQ as central coordination hub for data flow and model updates.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Data exchange via RabbitMQ AMQP protocol with open-source message broker. Interoperability with external systems through GitHub-hosted hardware/software repository. Integration patterns include co-simulations with external PT data and potential enterprise system connectivity via Raspberry Pi's built-in Wi-Fi/Bluetooth. Dependencies on external sensors (DS18S20) and actuators (fan) for physical twin interaction.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Digital Twin models include behavioral and analytical models (e.g., equations for energy flow: E_heater = V I ∆t − E_heater2air, dT_heater/dt = (V I ∆t − G_heater(T_heater−T_bair))/C_heater). Data sources are sensors (e.g., DS18S20 temperature sensors) and physical twin operations. Models are calibrated using nonlinear least squares optimization (Scipy package) to minimize residual error. Model B (4 parameters: C_heater, C_air, G_heater, G_box) provides higher fidelity for thermal inertia prediction. Data management involves calibration algorithms, time series databases, and co-simulation with historical/real-time data from the physical twin.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators include MOSFETs for controlling 4A current to the heatbed, a fan for airflow regulation, and a heatbed as the thermal actuator. Control mechanisms use Raspberry Pi GPIO via optocouplers to isolate control signals. Actuation ranges are limited by MOSFET current capacity (4A) and safety constraints (e.g., thermal protection). Remote control interfaces include RabbitMQ for command transmission between controller and low-level drivers.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are DS18S20 High-Precision 1-Wire Digital Thermometers (accuracy: ±0.5°C in -10°C to +85°C range, -55°C to +125°C operational). Three sensors are spatially distributed for temperature monitoring. Sampling frequency not explicitly stated, but data transmission uses 1-Wire protocol with serial codes (64-bit) for multi-sensor daisy-chaining. Data collection mechanisms include low-level drivers interfacing with RabbitMQ for real-time telemetry.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity requires parameter calibration (e.g., C_air=486.12 J/K, G_box=0.856 J/K for Model B). Error detection uses Kalman filters for heatbed state estimation. Quality assurance involves iterative calibration, residual error minimization (Scipy optimization), and co-simulation with real-time data for what-if scenarios.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data flows from physical sensors (DS18S20 temperature sensors) to digital twin via 1-Wire protocol. Data types include temperature readings (-55°C to +125°C, 0.5°C accuracy). Communication protocol: RabbitMQ message broker for real-time data transmission. Transmission frequency: Continuous monitoring with periodic updates. Event triggers: Temperature thresholds or calibration events. Data quality assurance: Sensor calibration and model validation (e.g., Model B parameters C_air=486.12 J/K, G_box=0.856 J/K). Preprocessing steps: Data normalization for simulation models. Data integration mechanisms: RabbitMQ as middleware for synchronizing sensor data with simulation models.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control flows from digital twin to physical components via RabbitMQ. Command types: Fan speed control (12V/24V), heatbed activation, and temperature regulation. Control message formats: JSON-based commands through RabbitMQ. Validation mechanisms: Optocouplers decouple control signals from Raspberry Pi GPIO. Feedback systems: Real-time temperature sensor data for closed-loop control. extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication protocols: RabbitMQ (AMQP) for message brokering, 1-Wire for sensor data. Network topology: Edge-based (Raspberry Pi 4) with sensors/actuators connected via GPIO and optocouplers. Bandwidth requirements: Low-latency (<2s for fan air circulation). extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Hosting platform: Edge (Raspberry Pi 4 Model B) with onboard Wi-Fi/Bluetooth. Computational requirements: ARM Cortex-A53 processor for real-time control and simulation. Storage: On-device storage for time-series data (e.g., temperature logs). Scalability: Limited to single-node deployment; no cloud integration described. Deployment models: Standalone edge deployment with open-source software (RabbitMQ, PCB schematics). Availability assurance: Redundant sensors and fail-safe shutdown protocols for uninterrupted operation.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: Development stages include model calibration (models A/B with 2/4 parameters), integration into Digital Twin framework, and self-adaptation loop for dynamic reconfiguration. Representation types transition from simulation models to real-time data integration. Lifecycle governance involves continuous calibration, model reconfiguration, and anomaly-driven experiments. Transition management includes phased deployment of models and system reconfiguration upon detecting plant behavior changes.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Engineering processes use RabbitMQ for real-time communication, model calibration (models A/B with parameters C_air, G_box, C_heater, G_heater), and version control via open-source repositories. Evolution strategies include recalibration of Model B, Kalman filter reconfiguration, and co-simulation for what-if scenarios. Testing validates models through experiments (e.g., 18V fan tests) and simulation accuracy verification. Quality assurance practices involve parameter optimization and anomaly detection validation.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support includes real-time temperature visualization via dashboards, anomaly detection using Kalman filters correlating sensor data to heatbed states, and what-if simulations for controller optimization. Analytics process sensor data (DS18S20) and simulation outputs. User interaction involves dashboard visualization and automated controller reconfiguration. Confidence measures derive from model calibration accuracy (e.g., Model B's inertia prediction) and Kalman filter state estimation reliability.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

### **6 Summary**


We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware,
software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology,
we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.

specifications of the case study.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

Although the dynamics models have been built, they contain free parameters. In order

the calibration are detailed in section 4.2, and here we detail the procedure.
A non-linear least squares solver was used to calibrate the parameters of the models
derived above. Then the models are evaluated using the estimated parameters. Afterwards the resulting behaviour is compared with a data trajectory that is obtained by
running an experiment of the incubator. Finally using an optimisation function inside
the python Scipy package [14] to obtain the values of the parameters. Such a function
finds the parameters that minimizes the residual error. When the residual error is small
enough, the optimised parameters can be obtained.
The principle of the package is based on least-square, it assumes that:


_y_ = _f_ ( _x, θ_ ) + _ϵ_ (10)


where _f_ ( _x, θ_ ) is the model needed to be calibrated, _θ_ are the parameters represented in
a vector in the model, and _y_ is the label or the measured data.
A cost function or an objective function can be provided as:


_J_ = �( _y_ _i_ _−_ _f_ ( _x_ _i_ _, θ_ )) [2] (11)


The objective is to acquire a minimal value of _J_, and at the same time, the corresponding _θ_ are the value needed. In order to minimise the cost function, the most commonly
used one is gradient descent. After multiple iterations, the value of the cost function
goes to a sufficient small value and the _θ_ are obtained simultaneously.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,

University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology,

**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary**

**3.3** **Calibration**


Although the dynamics models have been built, they contain free parameters. In order
to utilise the models, the models need to be calibrated for the incubator. The results of
the calibration are detailed in section 4.2, and here we detail the procedure.
A non-linear least squares solver was used to calibrate the parameters of the models
derived above. Then the models are evaluated using the estimated parameters. Afterwards the resulting behaviour is compared with a data trajectory that is obtained by
running an experiment of the incubator. Finally using an optimisation function inside
the python Scipy package [14] to obtain the values of the parameters. Such a function
finds the parameters that minimizes the residual error. When the residual error is small
enough, the optimised parameters can be obtained.
The principle of the package is based on least-square, it assumes that:


_y_ = _f_ ( _x, θ_ ) + _ϵ_ (10)


where _f_ ( _x, θ_ ) is the model needed to be calibrated, _θ_ are the parameters represented in
a vector in the model, and _y_ is the label or the measured data.
A cost function or an objective function can be provided as:


_J_ = �( _y_ _i_ _−_ _f_ ( _x_ _i_ _, θ_ )) [2] (11)


The objective is to acquire a minimal value of _J_, and at the same time, the corresponding _θ_ are the value needed. In order to minimise the cost function, the most commonly
used one is gradient descent. After multiple iterations, the value of the cost function
goes to a sufficient small value and the _θ_ are obtained simultaneously.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary**

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

### **6 Summary**


We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware,
software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology,
we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

To do this, a DS18S20 High-Precision 1-Wire Digital Thermometer (see fig. 3(e)) was used for measuring the temperature. This temperature sensor can measure temperatures from -55 °C to +125 °C with an accuracy of 0.5 °C from -10 to +85 °C, which is suitable for the physical incubator. And each DS18S20 has a unique 64-bit serial code, which allows multiple DS18S20s to function on the same 1-Wire bus. Such 1-wire bus support to connect multiple sensors with only one GPIO. In the incubator system, three sensors were deployed for detecting the temperature. **2.1.6** **The PCB Design** Controlling the heatbed requires approximately 4A current which is not supported by the Raspberry Pi. In order to decouple the control signals from the Raspberry Pi to the MOSFETs, we use optocouplers. The PCB schematic is shown in fig. 4.
